articles.desc: Background tree mortality is the ambient, natural death of trees that occurs without major disturbances. articles.desc: Incubation experiments reveal that potentially carbon‐rich saline permafrost and cryopegs from Arctic coastal plains exhibit CO2 production potentials comparable to active layers. articles.desc: Grassland recovery following nutrient cessation is asymmetric and strongly shaped by nutrient enrichment history. articles.desc: We monitored greenhouse gas emissions from a Mediterranean reservoir over 2 years that differed markedly in water availability. Methane emissions were much higher during the drier year, largely because lower water levels and warmer bottom waters favored methane production and release, while carbon dioxide emissions were less affected. articles.title: Contrasting Warming Responses of Crops Harvested for Aboveground and Belowground Organs: A Global‐Meta Analysis
articles.desc: Global warming exacerbates heat stress and threatens food production, but its effects vary by crop type and harvested organ. Our results reveal that warming causes larger yield reductions in crops harvested for aboveground organs (CA) compared to those harvested for belowground organs (CB). articles.desc: Building on a 34‐year fertilization trial, global soil metagenomes and virus‐addition microcosms, we propose a soil Viral Entombing‐Priming (VEP) framework to describe the contrasting roles of viruses in carbon and nutrient dynamics depending on soil fertility. articles.desc: Soil freeze–thaw cycles (FTCs), which become more frequent and intense in permafrost regions under climate warming, profoundly promote soil carbon release to the atmosphere. Based on large‐scale sampling across the Tibetan alpine permafrost region, we found that the response of soil carbon release to FTCs was primarily associated with soil carbon availability. articles.title: Vegetation Increases CH4 Emissions and Methanotroph Diversity in Marine Sediments
articles.desc: Seagrass meadows enhance carbon burial but also increase methane (CH4) emissions from marine sediments. The presence of seagrass modifies sediment properties, creating carbon‐rich environments characterized by higher carbon accumulation and CO2 fluxes, which stimulate CH4 production and lead to higher emissions. articles.desc: Eddy covariance is often applied using a single tower to measure net ecosystem exchanges of gases and heat‐in other words, to infer the integrated source/sink strengths. articles.desc: Across the extratropical Northern Hemisphere, daytime and nighttime vapor pressure deficit (VPD) exert contrasting effects on vegetation productivity, and these effects depend strongly on water availability. articles.title: Land‐Use Effects on Surface Water Quality in Temperate Lowland Peatlands
articles.desc: We examined the effects of land use on UK lowland peat surface water chemistry, finding similar macronutrient stoichiometry at conservation‐managed and rewetted sites (indicative of nitrogen and phosphorus co‐limitation), suggesting that rewetting leads to a nutrient balance comparable to natural conditions. articles.title: Soil Microbial Diversity and Network Organization Respond to Land Use and Agricultural Inputs Worldwide
articles.desc: Based on a global analysis of 1921 soil samples, we show that agricultural management acts as a powerful ecological filter reshaping microbial communities. Land use and inputs—pesticides and fertilizers—drive a functional shift toward opportunistic pathogens and fragmented, less connected microbial networks, potentially compromising soil ecosystem resilience and stability on a global scale. articles.title: Individual Trees Respond to 40 Years of Climate Change Through Leaf Functional Trait Acclimation
articles.desc: We measured leaf traits from historic and contemporary herbarium specimens collected from the same individual trees in the southern Peruvian Amazon and compared trait changes to local changes in climate which included increasing temperature, decreasing precipitation, and increasing [CO2]. articles.title: Divergence From Temperate Models: Pollution Dominance and Stochastic Assembly in a Continental Freshwater System Under Compressed Modernity
articles.desc: A survey of 502 freshwater sites across China reveals that chemical pollution (COD, BOD, TN, TP) dominates biodiversity variation, overriding climatic and land‐use drivers. articles.title: Microbial Community Structure, Rather Than Diversity, Predicts Plant Yield Under Global Change
articles.desc: Global change reshapes plant–microbe interactions by altering microbial community assembly and network stability. Across 272 experimental comparisons, nitrogen addition showed the strongest effects on both plant yield and microbial communities. Plant adaptive performance was more strongly linked to microbial community structure than to microbial diversity, with higher yield associated with more complex microbial networks enriched in Proteobacteria and Actinobacteria. articles.desc: The findings suggest that reliance on above‐ground‐only measurements systematically overestimates the degree of overcompensation in grazed grasslands, underscoring the necessity of whole‐plant assessments for accurately evaluating grassland productivity. articles.title: Tree canopy shapes the survival of oak and beech saplings during the regeneration stage along an elevation‐driven temperature gradient
articles.desc: Forests are complex, multilayered systems in which canopy cover strongly influences understorey microclimates. Here, we show that the temperature‐buffering effect of the forest canopy affects tree regeneration differently among species along an elevation gradient in the French Pyrenees. These findings improve our understanding of species responses to climate change and have important implications for forest management and predictions of future species distributions under ongoing global warming.

articles.desc: Are high‐elevation plants still adapted to high elevations? We grew 23 populations from across a broad elevational and latitudinal range of a native CA wildflower at a high elevation garden. We found evidence for adapational lag in high‐elevation populations, but only during some life stages. articles.desc: Our results reveal that trait–growth relationships shift across water availability, neighbourhood density and soil biotas, challenging current vegetation models that rely on mean trait values. articles.title: Extending the leaf economics spectrum: Freshwater plant communities exhibit realm‐specific leaf adaptations
articles.desc: This study examines whether the leaf economics spectrum, a key framework linking plant functional traits to biodiversity and ecosystem functioning, applies to aquatic plants. Using data from almost 7000 plant communities in lakes and rivers across Europe, we show that emergent and submerged aquatic plants respond differently to environmental conditions, highlighting the need for realm‐specific trait frameworks. articles.title: Geographic variation in diaspore traits and home‐site advantage in ant‐mediated dispersal
articles.desc: The results demonstrate that ant‐mediated selection on diaspore traits varies geographically, possibly resulting in uneven expression of home‐site advantage across populations. This spatial heterogeneity is consistent with predictions from the geographic mosaic theory of coevolution and highlights the importance of evolutionary ecological context in shaping mutualistic interactions across a species' range. articles.title: Partitioning tree growth into light interception and use efficiencies clarifies the role of light competition in secondary forest succession
articles.desc: By decomposing tree growth into light interception and use efficiencies, this study reveals how light competition drives secondary forest succession. In young stands, the superior light capture of taller trees dominates, forcing rapid height stratification. In older stands, higher light use efficiency of shade‐tolerant species enables them to thrive under tall canopies, thereby promoting vertical species coexistence. articles.title: Higher temperatures and low precipitation strongly decrease conifer recruitment in the Rocky Mountains even in heat‐adapted species and populations
articles.desc: Climate change is increasing the likelihood of tree species recruitment failure in the Rocky Mountains, especially in the warmest and driest portions of forest distributions, although regeneration of more drought‐resistant species may prove resilient to hotter conditions in areas with sufficient precipitation. articles.desc: In classic trait‐based approaches, traits reflect different plant strategies that evolved over long‐term evolutionary timescales. The study indicates that these trade‐offs also emerge consistently across populations within single species, yielding similar ecological consequences. articles.desc: Widely studied stability drivers such as plant diversity and functional composition have only partial explanatory power for short‐term drought resistance of above‐ground productivity in grasslands at a global scale. articles.title: Cation responses to global changes alter ecological processes in ombrotrophic systems
articles.desc: Ombrotrophic bogs, which store large amounts of carbon as peat, receive all their mineral nutrition through deposition, making them particularly sensitive to cation‐displacing global changes. Here, we review the limited yet compelling evidence that cation availability is a key determinant of ecological processes across scales of ecological organisation in ombrotrophic bogs and propose a research agenda. articles.desc: This study reveals a dual response in grasslands to repeated drought, where community fine‐root traits maintain high resistance through species turnover. This suggests that community functional responses to climate extremes cannot be inferred from species trait shifts alone. articles.title: Generalist passerine birds perform a functional role as pollinators in temperate Europe
articles.desc: Nectar‐feeding birds are effective pollinators. In temperate Europe, songbirds visit flowers but lack specialist traits, so functional pollination has been discounted. We use observations, pollen identification and experiments to demonstrate that generalist birds affect fruit‐set of flowering trees. articles.title: Autumn nutrient resorption and twig storage shape spring phenology in woody species
articles.desc: Autumn nutrient resorption from senescing leaves and subsequent storage in twigs links internal nutrient cycling to spring phenology in woody species. Acquisitive species showed higher nutrient resorption and greater twig nutrient accumulation, which were associated with earlier bud break and longer leaf‐out duration. articles.title: Eco‐evolutionary shifts in interactions between a globally invasive plant and below‐ground putative pathogens coincide with shifts in plant performance
articles.desc: Differences in pathogen communities across ranges may have caused evolutionary changes in plant–pathogen associations in C. canadensis. articles.desc: Phylogenetic and geographic distances captured distinct dimensions of competition among Quercus congeners. articles.title: Non‐random species loss weakens functional diversity and species asynchrony, destabilizing grassland communities
articles.desc: Our findings demonstrate that grassland community stability is shaped more by the order of loss of species than by the total number of species lost and how these losses reorganize stabilizing processes. articles.title: Carbon currency mediates AM fungal–microbe interactions

articles.desc: Increasing precipitation reshapes plant–microbial nutrient partitioning and ecosystem C and N retention in alpine ecosystems. articles.desc: Habitat selection studies rarely account for fitness or health, yet our simulations show that ignoring such information can misguide management actions. Using three case studies, we demonstrate how easily health and fitness proxies can be integrated into habitat selection analyses and the added insights they provide, such as links between habitat selection and mass gain. articles.title: Extreme site fidelity in long‐distance migratory shorebirds in Australia and potential implications for conservation
articles.desc: Abstract Site fidelity is the tendency for animals to repeatedly return to the same locations, either within or between years. Site fidelity enables animals to utilize knowledge of previously visited locations, including assessments of seasonal variations in health and mortality risks (e.g., predation), resource availability, and social benefits such as pairing with previous mates. articles.title: Improving efficiency in conservation planning by modeling species‐specific responses to threat abatement
articles.desc: Abstract Reversing ongoing biodiversity loss requires effectively managing anthropogenic threats. Although conservation actions can mitigate these threats, prioritizing and targeting such actions remain challenging because species respond differently depending on the nature of the threats and their biological traits. articles.title: What climate adaptation can learn from evolutionary adaptation
articles.desc: Abstract Evolutionary and climate adaptation both describe how complex systems respond to environmental change, either via natural selection or deliberate human choices. Although adaptation in both contexts is expected to produce favorable outcomes, it can also result in maladaptation—ineffective, unintended, or harmful outcomes. articles.title: Key agroecosystems for the conservation of amphibians and reptiles in Europe
articles.desc: Abstract Agricultural expansion and intensification are driving amphibian and reptile declines worldwide. However, sustainably managed agroecosystems can support a high diversity of herpetofauna, which play a key role in pest control, reducing crop damage. Therefore, integrating herpetofauna conservation into agricultural policies is vital for enhancing human well‐being, but it remains largely overlooked. articles.title: Exploring legal‐ and health‐risk messaging to reduce demand for elephant skin
articles.desc: Abstract Asian elephants (Elephas maximus) are poached for an illegal trade in their skins, which are used in traditional medicine in Africa and Asia. We explored whether messages about the legal and health risks of using elephant skin for medicinal purposes (stomach illness) could reduce such consumption. articles.title: Nonlinear dynamics of fish beta diversity and the disproportionate influence of non‐native species in cascade‐dammed rivers
articles.desc: Abstract Cascade dam development is transforming freshwater ecosystems worldwide, yet the long‐term responses of fish beta diversity to progressive hydrological disturbances remain poorly understood. We analyzed data from the upper reaches of the Yellow River on the Qinghai–Tibet Plateau spanning five decades (pre‐1975 to 2023), across a 1983‐km river corridor influenced by sequential cascade dams. articles.title: Making conservation inclusive and count through globally important agricultural heritage systems as possible other effective area‐based conservation measures
articles.desc: Abstract Protected areas (PAs) are critical to mitigation of drastic biodiversity decline and habitat loss. Despite considerable global progress in PAs, future efforts need to overcome persistent challenges related to coverage and effectiveness. With limited capacity for expansion of PAs, achieving the targets of the Kunming–Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (KM‐GBF) will depend on other effective area‐based conservation measures (OECMs). articles.title: Identifying hotspots of faunal data deficiency to direct urgent research and monitoring
articles.desc: Abstract Data deficiency is a substantial challenge for extinction risk assessments because incomplete data means we cannot accurately identify priority protected areas for conservation. Here, we use the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) distributions of amphibians, sharks and rays, fish, mammals, birds, and reptiles to identify hotspots of data deficient species. articles.title: Trapped honey bees reduce floral visitation on milkweed flowers
articles.desc: Abstract Trapped arthropods have been shown to benefit plants in several ways, but few studies have examined the potential costs of arthropod entanglement. Milkweeds (Asclepias spp.) have an unusual pollination system that requires relatively large packets of pollen (pollinia) to become attached to the appendages of insect pollinators and be pulled through a narrow opening in the flower. articles.title: Species interactions shape the thermal performance curve and distribution of tropical Drosophila species
articles.desc: Abstract Decades of research have demonstrated the importance of temperature in dictating species distributional limits. We know species interactions also play a key role, but we lack a predictive framework for understanding when and where species interactions will be more important than temperature in shaping distributional limits. articles.title: Fuzzy relationships among plant dispersal mechanisms, syndromes, and animal vectors
articles.desc: Abstract Knowledge of which animal clades and dispersal mechanisms contribute to seed dispersal is critical for understanding the ecology and evolution of plant communities. However, a broad understanding is limited by a focus on single clades and mechanisms. articles.title: Consequences of phenological shifts are determined by the number of generations per season
articles.desc: Abstract The order and timing of species' arrival in a community can often change species interactions. Termed priority effects, this phenomenon arises in communities with drastically different life histories (e.g., bacteria, insects, and amphibians), yet how their life history traits affect the consequences of priority effects in seasonal systems remains understudied. articles.title: Ground‐to‐canopy monitoring reveals hidden ecological patterns in Congo Basin mammals
articles.desc: Abstract Vertical stratification has long been recognized as a key dimension of biodiversity in structurally complex ecosystems, shaping animal movement and community structure. Camera traps provide a powerful means to extend biodiversity monitoring across forest strata through continuous, standardized observation. articles.title: Mechanistic and scale‐specific analyses advance the preference–performance hypothesis
articles.desc: Abstract Oviposition choice and larval performance are essential determinants of fitness in plant–herbivore interactions. The long‐standing preference–performance hypothesis (PPH) predicts that parents should oviposit on plants that maximize offspring performance; an abundance of work supports the PPH, but many exceptions limit its generality. Here the authors show that disturbance is a widespread predictor of range disequilibrium in the vascular flora of Europe and determines whether plant species can occupy landscapes with suitable climates.

articles.desc: Nature Ecology & Evolution, Published online: 06 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41559-026-03122-1 A marine harmful algal bloom off coastal South Australia in 2025 persisted for more than a year, and affected an area of at least 20,000 km2, causing the deaths of millions of marine animals and affecting human health. We identified the source of the toxicity as Karenia cristata, which produces the neurotoxin brevetoxin.

Here we discuss how an article published in 2018 linked the buffering role of glaciers to future seasonal runoff losses, and how later work has extended this insight towards drought, water resources and the consequences for downstream societies.

articles.desc: Nature Plants, Published online: 01 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41477-026-02331-7 Chloroplasts have key roles in plant immunity, but how external immune signals reach these organelles is unclear. We identified TaMP, a wheat protein that relocalizes from the plasma membrane to chloroplasts in response to pathogen infection, and found that a rust effector hijacks this process to promote TaGluTR degradation and increase wheat susceptibility to rust.

articles.title: The cytosolic arginine pool represses the progression of leaf senescence
articles.desc: Nature Plants, Published online: 01 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41477-026-02330-8 How senescing cells time their transition to the cell death programme is unclear. We show that, in Arabidopsis thaliana, senescing cells are genetically wired to dissipate their cytosolic arginine pool. This pool serves as a proxy for the efficiency of nitrogen remobilization, enabling precise timing between the remobilization and termination phases of senescence.
